tokens: Waking you up an hour early because we've got a little problem on-board we need addressed.
When you get a moment to get something to write it down, let me talk to you about it in detail.
The problem we're looking at is a leak in Descent O2 and we're trying to determine whether we got a small cabin leak or a leak in the oxygen system itself.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: First thing we'd like is high-bit-rate.
That will allow us to look at some extra parameters, particularly temperature, to see how much of this fall is due to temperature effects, which we haven't experienced before (because of the SEVA).
So, if you're standing right there, why don't you flip that on while I read you the rest of the Pad, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Next step is Descent O2 (tank valve) to Close.

And why don't you do that now, because the step following that is for the ground to monitor the Descent O2 tank pressure and the cabin pressure; and we're going to look and see if the leak, which we've seen so far as a drop in the tank pressure, is due to a cabin leak or to a leak in the system itself.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Then, you might copy down the following steps to be performed, depending upon our analysis of what we're watching right now.
One, if leak stops, upper and forward hatch (dump) valves, Close.
(Pause) And ensure that the urine QD (Quick Disconnect) is capped.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We were just looking at the
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Copy and understand that the receptacle or transfer device was attached to the hose all night.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We'd like you to leave it in the configuration you found it in for a few minutes, because that will allow us to verify that that's where the leak was.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm going to open the (urine) valve again.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: (Pause) I'll tell you, that's good news if that's what it is, man.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Understand on your first...(stops to listen to Parker) Okay, on that first step that you read, it said, if leak stops, to close both
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: In other words, if we see that the leak has stopped here on the ground, we will then ask you to close both dump valves and also verifying the urine QD.

And, if the leak in the cabin - now, if the cabin is completely isolated - continues to hold, we will then ask you to open the hatch valves one at a time to verify that the hatch valves are not going to leak or which hatch valve is leaking in the open position.
Some of that will be not indicated (that is, not performed) if, indeed, it is the urine QD that's capped.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And we'd like to verify right now, just quickly, that the Descent O2 is Closed; that the urine receptacle is back in its original configuration.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Let's just stand by here for a couple of minutes and we'll see what the ground has to tell us.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Long Pause) Bob, as long as we're talking about consumables, what are you showing down there for water?
Because we are reading, oh, about 60 percent on descent 1 and 2 now.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We've got to read our decals down here, too.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, you did copy my question about the water quantity?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And we're having to read our decals down here, too.
It will take us a minute.
We'll be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Jim, we have an answer for you on your water gauge problem.
The 60 that you're seeing indeed corresponds to a true 70 percent; which, indeed, is the number that we were expecting to see.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The urine receptacle is off; and the other QD is installed.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We're standby to watch what comes off down here on the ground.
(Long Pause) And, Jim, we'll be sitting here in this configuration for about 5 minutes to watch the cabin again.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It looked like you were getting pretty good sleep there for a while, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It looked like you were getting a pretty good sleep there.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's the best sleep I've had on the flight.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I was way down in sleep when you gave us a call.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Let's get the problem squared away.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Yeah, I figured we lost a little bit of sleep down here on the ground tonight.
I couldn't even fall asleep at my console.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: If you're not too busy, I could copy the lift-off data for (Command Module orbits number) 21 to 27.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: If you're ready to copy, then: T-21 is 188:30:48.
(Pause as his error is pointed out to him) Negative; that should be a 118.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'll give you a quick readback on that.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And, if you have the consumables update, I'll take that, too.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We believe that...We are very strongly convinced, finally, down here, that that indeed solves the problem.
You can go back to Descent O2, Open.
And we'd like to suggest that the procedure, when using that particular device, from now on, will be to remove the resepricle [sic] when you're finished and cap it as we've just done.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And, gee, the sleeping is really good up here; and, if y'all ever see another little problem like that, why, we'd be only to happy to roll over and take care of it.
I think, as a matter of fact, we'd even sleep better if we knew that you wouldn't mind waking us.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: You can sleep better because now you know I can wake you up anyway.
The thing (Dave's earpiece) didn't drop out of your ear, apparently, Dave.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And you guys have about 22 minutes left (before the scheduled wake-up), if you want to go back to sleep again.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, I tell you, we probably could.
We were just talking it over, and both of us slept just as well in here as we do at home.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Well, frankly, Dave, it's your guys' option.
If you want to lay down and take a little morning snooze while the rest of us keep working down here, that's your option.
I'm not sure that we're talking about starting the EVA early.
I think that was kind of agreed upon before, [that] we start on time, wasn't it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We don't want to start the EVA early.
But I think we'll take advantage of the extra little bit of time here to keep getting organized.
It takes a little while to settle down and get a system here for living to be efficient; and I think we can make good use of the time.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Well, we both feel pretty well rested at this point.
And we'll just mush along here at a nice easy pace and, hopefully, be all ready by the time it's time to go out.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay; and when you're ready, you can give me call; when you're ready for some updates, particularly concerning EVA planning, and I'll have a few good words to pass you then.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We'll get breakfast cooking here, and give you a call.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We've got another little question here on your questioning last night (at
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I guess last night the question came down to whether we could read it well enough down here on the ground.
To the best of our ability down here to read this, you got a full charge, but apparently that's plus or minus about a pound (out of a 12-pound capacity of each PLSS).
But our readings down here, for what they're worth, say you got a good charge.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We felt like we did (get a good fill) by looking at the sight gauges, although on the first PLSS (Jim's), there were still a few little bubbles running through when we reached the Max time, by looking at the sight gauge.
And on the second PLSS, it was clear, oh, within 4 or 5 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: It looks like we can tell you that, at the present time, our extrapolations indicate we'll have sufficient oxygen for a completely nominal mission, including the PLSS recharge on EVA-3.
Having high enough pressure (in the descent stage oxygen tank) for the PLSS recharge was our hardest constraint, and it looks like we meet it, although rather closely.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, we'll breathe slowly and save as much as we can.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We've got another question for you since we're sitting here eating and looking around.
Did you all, by chance, get any figures on our descent rate at touchdown?

We're just taking a look out here, and it looks like we may have stroked a gear some (that is, compressed the shock absorber on the forward landing strut, the only one of the four that they can see); and, as near as we can recall, we were coming down about a foot per second when we got the contact, and we just wondered if you had any data on that yet.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We have an answer for you; which is that we were showing 0.7 feet per second up to touchdown (means contact).
And then at touchdown, it appears that, at the same point we got the roll and pitch, we picked up to something like 3.7 feet per second at that point.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: So we went from 0.7 to 3.7 from contact to touchdown.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, we're having to look at these based upon times.
We don't see the contact light, and we don't really know when touchdown occurred; but we're looking at things like the times.
You were coming down at 0.7 there, all the way to the very end; and, then, at the same time that we see the pitchup and the rollover, we see an increase suddenly to 3.7.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Like we might have stuck our rear paw [sic] in a crater back there somewhere, huh?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Looks like we might have stuck the rear pad in a crater back there somewhere, huh?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Either that, or you touched down on something and fell through.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Long Pause) Well, that says the landing radar is pretty good data then.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're ready to talk over the EVA plan with you, if you'd like.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: First of all, we'll talk about the changes in the traverse plan, which are very minimal.
But for your planning, we're now showing a LM location on the grid map in the coordinates of Bravo-Romeo 3 and 75.5.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're going to have to get in the ETB and pull the maps out.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Bob, will you give me the coordinates again?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And that over there near November (Crater).
Okay, to write down, the rest of this - for a while, anyway - is kind of just advisory.
This new location adds approximately 0.6 kilometers to the EVA-1 traverse and, therefore, about six minutes driving time.

However, that's only provisionary, of course; and our indications of a beautiful flat plain out there (from Dave's SEVA description, primarily the lack of rocks) may mean that we'll make up some of that time just in being able to drive faster than we were, perhaps, anticipating.
(Pause) If this is not the case...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, before you get too far into that "broad flat plain" out there, I hope we made it clear that there is a fairly good population of craters which we're going to probably have to drive around.
Even though there are no boulders, we're still going to have somewhat of a "wander factor" in avoiding, oh, the three- to four-meter craters.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, in order to keep the EVA total time to the maximum of seven hours, the six minutes has already been deleted from the activities at the LM at the end of the traverse.
So that's where we've taken up the slack, at the present time.
And the, beyond that, no further changes have been made to the EVA-1 timeline.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Extra activities we'd like you to include: We'd like the big glass ball you saw in the vicinity of the LM (after the SEVA at
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay...(Stops to listen to Parker) Bob, give us a couple of minutes after each of those comments to come back at you.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay, we were just discussing the frags around the LM.

We can see a number of interesting rocks out here, and we thought it might be better to wait until we got back to the LM (at the end of the EVA) to pick them up; and make sure we didn't disturb the surface around it.
Although, we can pick them up fairly quickly in the beginning.
I guess it's your choice: if you want to spend the time in the beginning or wait until we get back.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: My first flip comment there was "before you leave the Moon".

The second comment, on the selected samples, "should be taken at the crew's convenience at the end of EVA" was apparently intended by the geologists to mean selected samples of these black rocks and other interesting frags.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, do you specifically want us to pick up the glass ball and the black rocks before we start (the) EVA-1 (traverse)?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We'll put the glass ball at a higher priority, apparently, because they're worried that the glass ball might get lost once the area gets mucked up a little bit, whereas the black rocks will probably still be there.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And...I guess our understanding of the contingency sample is that it's supposed to be typical of the surface around, rather than an exotic.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I guess that's basically true...I guess that's basically true, Dave; however, they would like this little glass ball.
You could also put it separately in a bag before you leave with the Rover, or I guess they really wouldn't mind, since they know particularly what it is, if it was part of the contingency sample.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Let me talk to you about Rover status.
The additional distance - this 0.6 (km) - and your new position will not affect the electrical power profile.
It's really a very small and almost trivial distance (compared with the battery-supported potential of over 100 kilometers of driving).

And, secondly, the LM slope (means the tilt), the 9 degrees by 9 (means 6) degrees, is within the angles for which the (Rover) deployment is specified - which is 15 degrees - and also within the angles for which it's been tested.

And I guess a couple of days ago they did tests 15 degrees of pitch and 5 degrees of roll at Marshall (Space Flight Center in Huntsville Alabama, the NASA center which supervised the design and construction of the Rover by Boeing and its subcontractors), and 14 degrees of pitch and 14 degrees of roll.
And, right now, we will, in real time, have the mock-up at Marshall positioned corresponding to our pitchup of 9 and roll of 6 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's nice to hear, and it sounds like those fellows are planning ahead as usual.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And as far as checklist changes, we have none.
As far as TV plan updates, we have none.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And, sometime, Dave, the medics would like a status report, post sleep.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And, when you're ready, Falcon, we've got some consumables (update) for you.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: On the crew status, I guess we both got about 5 hours sleep, based on the time we went to bed and the time we got up; no medication; and we're in good shape.
And I guess you can go ahead with the consumables.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And how about a PDR or
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'll tell you, it would help us out if we could do that once a day.
You know they're stowed down in the suit pocket, and we've got to do some digging to get them.
We'll give them to you twice a day if you really need them, but it takes a little time.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I think they're only called out for once a day, we agree; and somehow, I guess, TransEarth - (correcting himself) or Translunar - we got in the habit of getting them from you at the time when we didn't really need them, like at sleep.
We got them from you at sleep last night; it's just that, in the checklist, it calls them out for right now.
But you're right, it is only a requirement once a day, and we did get it last night.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Our crew status report has them in the evening before bed.
Do you want them then, or do you want them in the morning?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: When does your crew status report have them?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay; the Flight Plan shows it in the post-sleep.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We'd like one this morning before the EVA, and the surgeons promise they'll look at it (that is, the requirement) today.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (With a touch of sarcasm) That's encouraging.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay, Bob, I'm ready to copy the consumables.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: O2 descent (tank)1, 78.5; tank 2, 78; O2 ascent 1, 99; tank 2, 99; H2O descent 1, 58; tank 2, 58.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Long Pause) Bob, I'm just looking at the descent water.
It looks like we're off about 12 percent.
Is that any concern down there?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We didn't just suddenly lose that.
That was TELMU's understanding of how we wanted the consumables updated.
We're having a discussion about that right now.
That was meant to be what your reading on board should be.
I guess, saying that if you're reading 58 percent, you're in good shape.
Whereas, there's a separate figure, which is a figure down here, which I guess is something like the 70 percent that I gave you a few minutes ago.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That seems to be a question of how TELMU interprets what the consumables update should be.
We may change that around and let you know later.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The actual on-board figures - not the gauge figures that you'll read, but the actual onboard figures for water - are, descent 1, 70.7 and tank 2, 68.5.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're about due to wake the computer up for a little while; and, if you're ready, we'll bring it out of its sleep period for a minute and then put it back to sleep - if you want to take a look at it.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger, Dave; that sounds good to us.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It looks like it's still with us.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: As we get started on the suiting here.
I wanted to make sure the biomed data was coming through clean to you, so we don't have any problems once we get the suits on.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Apparently, Jim's data is not very clean, and we're not getting your data at all, Dave.
Other than that, it looks beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, I guess I don't mean right now, because you shouldn't be getting any data right now.
What I mean, are the signals acceptable for computation of PLSS data; or, perhaps, if they're not, you can give us a suggestion so, before we get going here, we're sure that you've got good data.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: As a matter of fact, while the good surgeons are thinking that one out, Jim's going to plug in through the suit now, and you can check it out.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: That sounds good, and we'll be watching.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: You should be receiving it, and we'd appreciate a call as soon as you can verify it's good.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, it looks real good to us.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And I'll let you take a look at mine as soon as I get to the suit part.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Verify for us, please, that your biomed data is unplugged now.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, he's off the headset, Joe; but, yeah, it's unplugged.
We're getting him into the suit.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: What we'll do is get all hooked up to the suit, and then let you check it out, and then climb into the suit, just to make sure.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Joe, looking at a battery management change at about 118:05 (on checklist page 2-11).
Should we do it on that time?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I'll be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Falcon, regarding your question on battery management, we would like you to do it per the checklist on the time listed there, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Will you check the Commander's biomed?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Dave's EKG (electro-cardiogram) data looks clean as a whistle.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: He's going to complete the suiting-up.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And I'll get to the batteries shortly.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I'm going to press on with the battery management now.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And both ED batteries check at 37 (volts).
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We're down to the point of PLSS donning and with our mission timer turned off here to save the power.
How about giving us a hack on when we depress, relative to the time now, so we can keep track?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're starting the clock right now.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah, but give us a hack on the time at which we should depress relative to your time there in Houston.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Understand, and we'll be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, we're looking at about 10 minutes after 7:00 (7:10 a.m.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're looking towards the cabin depress at about 8:00 o'clock Houston time.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And, Dave and Jim, there's nothing magic about that number.
Any time around there would be beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, that's all we wanted; just to have a rough cut on it.
transcript.speaker: Scott
